response:
THE LAW OF MOORE, FORMULATED BY GORDON ECKERT MOORE IN 1965, PREDICTS THAT THE NUMBER OF TRANSISTORS ON A MICROPROCESSOR WILL DOUBLE EVERY 18 TO 24 MONTHS, LEADING TO EXPONENTIAL GROWTH IN COMPUTING POWER. THIS LAW HAS BEEN A DRIVING FORCE BEHIND THE RAPID ADVANCEMENT OF TECHNOLOGY OVER THE PAST DECADES. HOWEVER, AS WE APPROACH THE PHYSICAL LIMITS OF SILICON BASED TECHNOLOGY, IT BECOMES INCREASINGLY DIFFICULT TO MAINTAIN THIS RATE OF GROWTH. SCIENTISTS AND ENGINEERS ARE NOW EXPLORING ALTERNATIVE MATERIALS AND NEW ARCHITECTURES TO CONTINUE THE ADVANCEMENT OF COMPUTING POWER.

***
WHILE THE LAW OF MOORE MAY NOT BE A STRICTLY MATHEMATICAL LAW, IT HAS BEEN A POWERFUL METAPHOR FOR TECHNOLOGICAL PROGRESS. MANY EXPERTS BELIEVE THAT THE LAW WILL CEASE TO BE VALID IN THE NEXT 10 TO 20 YEARS AS WE REACH THE PHYSICAL LIMITS OF SILICON TECHNOLOGY. HOWEVER, IT IS IMPORTANT TO REMEMBER THAT TECHNOLOGY EVOLVES IN DIFFERENT WAYS, AND NEW INNOVATIONS MAY EMBRACE DIFFERENT APPROACHES TO COMPUTING POWER. THE END OF THE LAW OF MOORE DOES NOT NECESSARILY MEAN THE END OF TECHNOLOGICAL PROGRESS, BUT RATHER A SHIFT IN HOW WE DEVELOP AND USE COMPUTING POWER.
